assistant_output_excerpt:
Planning the full build first, then I’ll dispatch the first coder task with the exact file and validation requirements so implementation can proceed without back-and-forth.
The plan is set. I’m sending the first coder task to inspect the workspace and pin the exact Ruby/Rails generator command before any scaffolding happens.
The harness rejected the first spawn because full-history fork can’t be combined with an explicit agent type. I’m retrying with a fresh coder context and the full subtask embedded inline.
Reconnaissance is complete: the root is effectively empty, and the scaffold target is `Ruby 4.0.3` with `Rails 8.1.3`. I’m dispatching the in-place Rails generation next.
The scaffold is in place. Next I’m dispatching dependency and tooling setup so the app has `ruby_llm`, test stubbing, coverage, linting, security checks, and a reproducible bundle state before feature work starts.
I caught a version drift from the generated scaffold: the app is currently on `ruby-4.0.2` even though reconnaissance found `4.0.3` available. I’m sending a fix-up now so the project baseline matches the “newest Ruby available” requirement before feature work begins.
The baseline and gems are aligned now, including Ruby `4.0.3`. I’m sending the main feature implementation task next: routes, controllers, service layer, Tailwind/Hotwire UI, OpenRouter wiring, and the Minitest suite that hits the real `chat.ask` path through stubbing.
The main app build is running now. I asked the coder to determine the OpenRouter Claude Sonnet slug for 2026-04-24, implement the no-DB chat flow, and prove the `chat.ask` path with WebMock-backed Minitest coverage before reporting back.
The core app is implemented and the focused chat tests are green. I’m moving to the delivery layer now: CI/security tooling, README, and container setup so we can do full end-to-end validation after that.
The delivery layer is being wired now. I asked the coder to produce a real `bin/ci`, GitHub Actions workflow, Docker/Compose setup, and a README that explains local and container usage without leaking any secret-handling into the repo.
The stack is already building and passing its local CI script, which is a strong signal. I’m still doing the explicit convergence pass now to catch any cross-file contract drift before I ask for the final validation matrix again end-to-end.
The convergence audit spawn was blocked by the harness’s open-agent limit. I’m closing the completed workers and then I’ll re-run the audit task cleanly.
The convergence pass found two leftover generated assumptions and cleaned them. I’m re-running the full validation matrix now under `mise exec ruby@4.0.3`, including local boot and Docker Compose, so the final state is post-fix and not based on earlier results.
The final matrix is running against the post-fix state now. I asked the coder to use `mise exec ruby@4.0.3` for every Ruby command and to re-check both local boot and `docker compose up --build`, not rely on earlier pre-audit results.
Built the Rails app in the workspace root on Ruby `4.0.3` / Rails `8.1.3`. It’s a no-DB Tailwind + Hotwire chat SPA with session-backed transcript state, `ruby_llm` wired to OpenRouter, and `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6` as the default Claude Sonnet model as of April 24, 2026. The deliverables are in place, including [README.md](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project/README.md), [Dockerfile](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project/Dockerfile), [compose.yaml](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project/compose.yaml), [bin/ci](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project/bin/ci), and the chat service/controller/view/test stack.

Validation passed end-to-end in the final post-fix state:
- `mise exec ruby@4.0.3 -- bin/ci` passed.
- Local boot via `bin/rails server` responded `200 OK` on `/up`.
- `docker build
command: codex exec --json --ephemeral --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --skip-git-repo-check -s danger-full-access -C /mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project -m gpt-5.4 -c model_reasoning_effort=xhigh -c 'agents.coder.config_file="/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_4_multi_balanced_forced/project/.codex-coder.toml"' -c 'agents.coder.description="Use for all concrete coding execution dispatched by the planner-orchestrator."' -

codex_subagent.description: Use for all concrete coding execution dispatched by the planner-orchestrator.
codex_subagent.name: coder
codex_subagent.prompt: You are a focused coding agent. Execute the dispatched subtask exactly as specified. Follow the provided instructions, file paths, and acceptance criteria literally. If the subtask is ambiguous, pick the most reasonable interpretation and note it in your reply. Keep changes minimal — only do what the subtask describes. Match existing codebase style.

model.label: GPT 5.4 xHigh + medium coder (FORCED delegation)
model.selection_reason: Forced-delegation variant of gpt_5_4_multi_balanced. Same config as the free-choice version — the only difference is the forcing prompt at prompts/benchmark_prompt_forced_delegation.txt. Measures whether forcing the orchestrator pattern changes output quality or cost on Codex's multi_agent feature.
